messages.content:
correct and reformulate: "Perceiving the anodization scan rate changes that occurred in the chemistry of anodized Cu foils, their nanostructural properties were also extensively evaluated with scanning electron microscopy microscopy (SEM ). SEM micrographs for scan rate 3, 0.5 and 0.1 mV/s with increasing magnifications were obtained and are shown in Figure 3. The low-magnification SEM images for scan rate 3, 0.5 and 0.1 mV/s  clearly indicate the change in the shape and forme of oxide/hydroxide  in surface as expected from the increasing anodization scan rate. Especially, the Cu foil that was anodized in 3 mV/s showed only triangle  arrays with a size about 1 micrometer witch not cover all the surface. When the scan rate anodization was switched to 0.5 mV/s, a clear mixte of nanoneedles and triagles of Cu2O/Cu(OH)2. The triagle average size ∼0.7 μm was observed and the average length of nanoneedle is about 0.5 micrometer.
For scan rate 0.1 mV/s, only miconeedle covred the oxide surface with dense morphology . The average size of microneedle is  1.5 micrometer. Hence, from SEM observations, it can be concluded that the increasing in scan rate anodization decreases the average length of nano/microneedles of the Cu(OH)2 proportion of growth. And can change the oxide phases of copper oxide Cu(OH)2 to Cu2O. The SEM observation also confirmed the XRD analysis."

The changes in the chemistry of anodized Cu foils were observed by analyzing the anodization scan rate. Additionally, the nanostructural properties were assessed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Figure 3 displays the SEM micrographs at different magnifications for scan rates of 3, 0.5, and 0.1 mV/s. The low-magnification SEM images clearly show the alteration in shape and formation of oxide/hydroxide on the surface, which aligns with the increase in anodization scan rate. Specifically, the Cu foil anodized at 3 mV/s exhibited only triangular arrays with a size of approximately 1 micrometer, which did not cover the entire surface. When the anodization scan rate was decreased to 0.5 mV/s, Cu2O/Cu(OH)2 nanoneedles and triangles were observed. The average size of the triangles was approximately 0.7 μm, while the average length of the nanoneedles measured around 0.5 micrometers. For a scan rate of 0.1 mV/s, only microneedles were observed, forming a dense morphology on the oxide surface. The average size of the microneedles was 1.5 micrometers. Therefore, based on the SEM observations, it can be concluded that increasing the anodization scan rate results in a decrease in the average length of nano/microneedles and a change in the oxide phases from Cu(OH)2 to Cu2O. These findings were further supported by XRD analysis, as confirmed by the SEM observations.

The chemical changes in anodized Cu foils were investigated in relation to the scan rate of anodization. Furthermore, the nanostructural properties were extensively examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Figure 3 presents the SEM micrographs at different magnifications for scan rates of 3, 0.5, and 0.1 mV/s. The low-magnification SEM images clearly demonstrate the noticeable alterations in the shape and composition of the oxide/hydroxide on the surface, as anticipated with the increasing anodization scan rate. Specifically, the Cu foil anodized at 3 mV/s exhibited only triangular arrays, each with a size of approximately 1 micrometer, which did not cover the entire surface. When the scan rate was reduced to 0.5 mV/s, a mixture of Cu2O/Cu(OH)2 nanoneedles and triangles was clearly observed. The triangles had an average size of approximately 0.7 μm, and the nanoneedles had an average length of about 0.5 micrometer. In the case of a scan rate of 0.1 mV/s, only microneedles were present on the oxide surface, forming a densely packed morphology. The microneedles had an average size of 1.5 micrometers. Consequently, the SEM analysis indicates that an increase in the scan rate of anodization leads to a reduction in the average length of nano/microneedles in the Cu(OH)2 growth and a transformation of copper oxide phases from Cu(OH)2 to Cu2O. The results obtained from SEM observations were further supported by the XRD analysis.

correct and refolmulate :"Electrochemical characterization of copper oxide:
To evaluate the potential applications in electrochemical capacitors, the preparation simple by anodization with different morphologies were used to make supercapacitor electrodes, and characterized with cyclic voltammetry, galvanostatic charge/discharge and impedance measurements in Fig. 4. As shown in Fig. 4a, c and e, CV curves of the three simple  electrodes with different morphologies measured in a large scan rate range from 5 to 200 mV s-1 in the potential range 0.3-0.8 V vs. Hg/HgO."

The electrochemical properties of copper oxide were assessed for their suitability in electrochemical capacitors. Simple supercapacitor electrodes with varying morphologies were prepared through anodization and subjected to cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge/discharge, and impedance measurements, as depicted in Figure 4. Figure 4a, c, and e illustrate the CV curves of the three electrode samples with different morphologies, recorded across a wide range of scan rates from 5 to 200 mV/s within the potential range of 0.3-0.8 V versus Hg/HgO.

In order to assess the potential application of copper oxide in electrochemical capacitors, simple supercapacitor electrodes were prepared using different anodization techniques to obtain various morphologies. These electrodes were then subjected to various electrochemical characterization techniques, including cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge/discharge, and impedance measurements, as shown in Figure 4. The CV curves for the three electrode samples with different morphologies were recorded over a wide range of scan rates, ranging from 5 to 200 mV/s, within the potential range of 0.3-0.8 V versus Hg/HgO.

correct and refolmulate : "To study the potential applications in electrochemical capacitors, the preparation simples by LSV technique  with different scan rates were used to make supercapacitor electrodes, and characterized with cyclic voltammetry, galvanostatic charge/discharge and impedance measurements in Fig. 4. As shown in Fig. 4a, c and e, CV curves of the three simple  electrodes with different scan rates measured in a large scan rate range from 5 to 200 mV s-1 in the potential windows of 0.5 V vs. Hg/HgO using a flat cell contain 250 ml of 1M KOH. "

In order to investigate the potential applications of electrochemical capacitors, supercapacitor electrodes were fabricated using various scanning rates through the LSV technique. These electrodes were subsequently characterized using cyclic voltammetry, galvanostatic charge/discharge, and impedance measurements, as shown in Figure 4. The CV curves of the three different electrodes, prepared using different scan rates, were recorded over a wide scan rate range of 5 to 200 mV/s within a potential window of 0.5 V versus Hg/HgO. The measurements were conducted using a flat cell containing 250 ml of 1M KOH electrolyte.

correct and refolmulate: "The CV profiles for all the three samples were very similar and quite different from the ideal rectangular shape for double layer capacitance, and The unclear oxidation peaks and a broad reduction peak show nonrectangular CV curves due to reactions quasi-reversible faradaic process The different mass specific current densities indicated that the three samples had different specific capacitance"

The cyclic voltammetry (CV) profiles of the three samples exhibited resemblances, deviating from the expected rectangular shape characteristic of double-layer capacitance. The absence of distinct oxidation peaks and the presence of a broad reduction peak indicated non-rectangular CV curves, pointing towards quasi-reversible faradaic reactions. Moreover, the varying mass-specific current densities indicated different specific capacitance values for the three samples.

The CV profiles of all three samples displayed similar characteristics, deviating from the expected rectangular shape associated with double-layer capacitance. Instead, they exhibited unclear oxidation peaks and a broad reduction peak, indicating non-rectangular CV curves attributed to quasi-reversible faradaic processes. These observations suggest the occurrence of additional redox reactions within the system. Furthermore, the different mass-specific current densities observed among the three samples indicate variations in their specific capacitance, highlighting differences in their electrochemical performance.

correct and refolmulate: "During the negative-going scan, the peak at 0.63 V is indicative of reduction process. The charge storage mechanism of a pseudocapacitive material can be expressed by plotting peak currents as a function of scan rates from cyclic voltammetry experiment. For surface redox reaction the peak current varies directly with scan rates and for a semi-infinite bulk diffusion process the peak current varies as square root of scan rates [ref]. Fig.5 shows the peak current as a function of square root of scan rate for the three simples micro-supercapacitors. The plots show linear relationship between peak currents and square root of scan rate, which suggests semi-infinite bulk diffusion controlled process"

The reduction process is indicated by the peak observed at 0.63 V during the negative-going scan. The underlying charge storage mechanism of a pseudocapacitive material can be understood by plotting the peak currents against the scan rates in cyclic voltammetry experiments. In the case of a surface redox reaction, the peak current is directly proportional to the scan rate, whereas for a semi-infinite bulk diffusion process, the peak current is proportional to the square root of the scan rate (ref). Figure 5 illustrates the relationship between peak current and the square root of the scan rate for the three different micro-supercapacitor samples. The plots exhibit a linear correlation between peak currents and the square root of the scan rate, suggesting that the process is governed by a semi-infinite bulk diffusion mechanism.

correct and refolmulate:"The areal capacitance the three simples micro-supercapacitors with different scan rates 0.1, 0.5, and 3 mV/s have been calculated using equation (1). Fig. 6C shows areal capacitances as a function of scan rates (5-200 mV/s ) for all samples. In all the samples areal capacitance decreases with increasing scan rate from 10 mVs-1 to 200 mVs-1. The areal capacitance of micro-supercapacitor increases with decrease in scan rate because as scan rate decreases the electrolyte ions has longer time to enter in the bulk of electrode surface, but at higher scan rate electrolyte ion movement limited to solely surface of electrode (ref). The areal capacitances of 0.1; 0.5, and 3 mV/s  are 38 mF cm-2 , 16 mF cm-2, and 17 mF cm-2, obtained at scan rate of 50 mVs-1. These value for the areal capacitance is much higher than bulk CuO supercapacitor (0.12 - 25 mF cm-2) (ref) and several carbon based microsupercapacitors such as porous carbon (1.5-3.5 mF cm-2 ) ref, onion like carbon (1.7 mF cm-2) ref, copper oxide nanosheet (0.12 mF cm−2) ref. reduced graphene (0.08 mFcm-2), laser scribed graphene (2.32 mF cm-2) and laser written reduced graphene (0.51 mF cm-2 ). The obtained specific capacitance values are comparable with or better than several oxide based micro-supercapacitor such as VN-NiO (1.85 mFcm-2), hydrous RuO2 (40.7 mF cm-2) , MnO2 (56.3 mF cm-2) and NPG/ MnO2 ( 7.1 mF cm-2).
Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) procedure was used to evaluate the electrochemical performance of the three simple micro-supercapacitor. Fig.7A shows GCD curves of micro-supercapacitors at 1 mAcm-2 in potential range of 0.3V to 0.8V. The nonlinear shape of GCD curves (Fig. 7A) reveals the features of pseudocapacitance, which are in conformity with the result of the CV curves (Fig 6A). The capacitance obtained from GCD at 2 mA cm−2 are 31 mF cm−2, 10 mF cm−2, and 8 mF cm−2 for nanostructures grown at 0.1 mVs−1, 0.5 mV s−1, and 3 mVs−1, respectively, in good agreement with the CV results. The specific capacitance calculated by GCD at different current densities, 2 to 20 mA cm−2, is shown in Fig. 11.(f). The maximum specific capacitance of 31 mF cm−2 is obtained at 2 mA cm−2 in the case of the sample obtained at 0.1 mV s−1, which is in agreement with the specific capacitance calculated from the cyclic voltammetry. The higher specific capacitance values at lower current densities are attributed to the longer time available to the electrolyte ions to access the bulk of the active electrode materials and lower potential drop compared to higher current density [ref]. This significant difference in the specific capacitance can be attributed to the difference in the morphology of surface of the three simple. The morphology may affect the specific capacitance of copper oxide in three aspects: the electrical resistance, the electrolyte diffusion within CuO nanostructures and the specific surface area of CuO [29]. The bundle-like Cu(OH)2 is longer and thus have larger electrical resistance than nanocube/bandle-like Cu2O/Cu(OH)2. The increasing order of the specific capacitance (nanocub/bundle-like Cu2O/Cu(OH)2 < bundl-like  Cu(OH)2) was consistent with increasing sequence of Cu(OH)2 specific surface area, indicating that the highly mesoporous structure and high specific surface area of the electrode facilitates the ions to transfer into the porous structure more easily which would lead to more redox faradic reactions and surface adsorption of electrolyte cations."

The areal capacitance of the three different micro-supercapacitors with scan rates of 0.1, 0.5, and 3 mV/s was calculated using equation (1). Figure 6C depicts the areal capacitance as a function of scan rates ranging from 5 to 200 mV/s for all samples. In all cases, the areal capacitance decreases with increasing scan rate from 10 mVs-1 to 200 mVs-1. The decrease in areal capacitance with higher scan rates can be attributed to the limited movement of electrolyte ions mainly confined to the electrode's surface, as opposed to when the scan rate is lower. As the scan rate decreases, the longer time allows electrolyte ions to infiltrate the bulk of the electrode's surface. The obtained areal capacitance values for the scan rates of 0.1, 0.5, and 3 mV/s are 38 mF cm-2 , 16 mF cm-2, and 17 mF cm-2, respectively, measured at a scan rate of 50 mVs-1. These values are significantly higher than those of bulk CuO supercapacitors (0.12 - 25 mF cm-2) and several carbon-based microsupercapacitors (such as porous carbon ranging from 1.5 to 3.5 mF cm-2, onion-like carbon at 1.7 mF cm-2, copper oxide nanosheet at 0.12 mF cm-2, reduced graphene at 0.08 mF cm-2, laser scribed graphene at 2.32 mF cm-2, and laser written reduced graphene at 0.51 mF cm-2). The obtained specific capacitance values are comparable to or even superior to various oxide-based micro-supercapacitors such as VN-NiO (1.85 mF cm-2), hydrous RuO2 (40.7 mF cm-2), MnO2 (56.3 mF cm-2), and NPG/MnO2 (7.1 mF cm-2).

To evaluate the electrochemical performance of the three micro-supercapacitor samples, galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) measurements were conducted. Figure 7A displays the GCD curves of the micro-supercapacitors at a current density of 1 mA/cm2 within the potential range of 0.3V to 0.8V. The non-linear shape of the GCD curves indicates the presence of pseudocapacitance, which aligns with the observations from the CV curves (Figure 6A). The capacitance calculated from GCD at a current density of 2 mA/cm2 is 31 mF cm-2, 10 mF cm-2, and 8 mF cm-2 for the samples prepared at scan rates of 0.1 mV/s, 0.5 mV/s, and 3 mV/s, respectively, which is consistent with the CV results. The specific capacitance determined at different current densities, ranging from 2 to 20 mA/cm2, is depicted in Figure 11(f). The highest specific capacitance of 31 mF cm-2 is obtained at a current density of 2 mA/cm2 for the sample prepared at a scan rate of 0.1 mV/s, in agreement with the specific capacitance obtained from cyclic voltammetry. The higher specific capacitance values observed at lower current densities can be attributed to the increased time available for electrolyte ions to access the bulk of the active electrode material and the reduced potential drop compared to higher current densities. This significant difference in specific capacitance can be attributed to the differences in surface morphology between the three samples. The morphology is believed
